steps.title: Determine what "reasonable success" requires
steps.content: The program captured wild condors to breed them and reintroduce them. For the biologists' claim of success to hold, the data must show the wild population growing — ideally surpassing the captive population. Look for the choice that captures an overall increase in wild condors.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The number of captive California condors steadily decreased between 2014 and 2020.': The captive population fluctuated slightly and stayed near 180-200 — it did not steadily decrease. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The numbers of California condors living both in captivity and in the wild decreased only slightly from 2019 to 2020.': A one-year dip in both counts does not demonstrate program success. ✗
  • Choice C — 'The difference between the number of captive and wild California condors remained relatively steady between 2014 and 2017.': The wild population grew while captive stayed flat, so the gap widened — not steady. ✗
  • Choice D — 'The number of wild California condors increased overall from 2014 to 2020 and exceeded the number of those living in captivity.': Wild condors grew from ~230 to ~330 and surpassed the captive count (~180) by 2020 — direct evidence of reintroduction success. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
